The typical state of something, or something that is typical.
A specific good or service (e.g. a drink) that someone typically orders.
examples: I'll just have the usual.

definitions: Most commonly occurring; typical.
examples: It is becoming more usual these days to rear children as bilingual.

definitions: Freedom from some business or activity.
Free time given over to a specific purpose; occupation, activity.
examples: The Conservative Party’s vacation of the centre ground gave an opportunity to its opponents.

definitions: To spend or take a vacation.
examples: This year, we’re vacationing in Mexico.

definitions: An elongated depression between hills or mountains, often with a river flowing through it.
The area which drains into a river.
The quality (positive or negative) that renders something desirable or valuable.
The degree of importance given to something.
examples: The Shakespearean Shylock is of dubious value in the modern world.
The value of my children's happiness is second only to that of my wife.

definitions: To estimate the value of; judge the worth of something.
To fix or determine the value of; assign a value to, as of jewelry or art work.
examples: I will have the family jewels valued by a professional.
Gold was valued highly among the Romans.

definitions: An instance of having won a competition or battle or succeeded in an effort.
The condition of having succeeded in a conflict or other effort.
examples: It was a great victory on the battlefield.

definitions: To achieve a victory
A rural habitation of size between a hamlet and a town.
A rural habitation that has a church, but no market.
examples: There are 2 churches and 3 shops in our village.

definitions: A musical four-string instrument, generally played with a bow or by plucking the string, with the pitch set by pressing the strings at the appropriate place with the fingers; also any instrument of the violin family.
A violinist.
examples: The first violin often plays the lead melody lines in a string quartet.

definitions: To play on, or as if on, a violin.
Someone who visits someone else; someone staying as a guest.
Someone who pays a visit to a specific place or event; a sightseer or tourist.
A usually alphabetized and explained collection of words e.g. of a particular field, or prepared for a specific purpose, often for learning.
The collection of words a person knows and uses.
examples: My Russian vocabulary is very limited.
The vocabulary of social sciences is often incomprehensible to ordinary people.

definitions: A game played on a rectangular court between two teams of two to six players which involves striking a ball back and forth over a net.
The inflated ball used in such a game.
A formalized choice on matters of administration or other democratic activities
An act or instance of participating in such a choice, e.g., by submitting a ballot
examples: One occasion indicative votes were used was in 2003 when MPs were presented with seven different options on how to reform the House of Lords.
The Supreme Court upheld the principle of one person, one vote.

definitions: To cast a vote; to assert a formalized choice in an election
To choose or grant by means of a vote, or by general consent
examples: Did you vote last month?
The depository may vote shares on behalf of investors who have not submitted instruction to the bank.

definitions: Someone who votes.
examples: In your opinion, should we allow 16 and 17 year olds to become voters?

definitions: The part of the body between the pelvis and the stomach.
A part of a piece of clothing that covers the waist.
A male or female attendant who serves customers at their tables in a restaurant, café or similar.
Someone who waits for somebody or something; a person who is waiting.
examples: Waiter! There's a fly in my soup.

definitions: The act of waking, or state of being awake.
The state of forbearing sleep, especially for solemn or festive purposes; a vigil.
(often followed by up) To stop sleeping.
(often followed by up) To make somebody stop sleeping; to rouse from sleep.
examples: I woke up at four o'clock this morning.
The neighbour's car alarm woke me from a strange dream.

definitions: A small case, often flat and often made of leather, for keeping money (especially paper money), credit cards, etc.
(by extension) A person's bank account or assets.
examples: The thief stole all the money and credit cards out of the old man's wallet.
It's unknown if the pro running back's recent sex scandal will hit him in the wallet or not.

definitions: A flow of water over the edge of a cliff.
A waterfall-like outpouring of liquid, smoke, etc.
examples: A waterfall of mist came from the open freezer.
Hey man, can I take a waterfall from your bottle?

definitions: To fall like a waterfall.
To drink (something) from a container by pouring it from a height so as not to touch one's lips to the rim.
A plant of the species Citrullus lanatus, bearing a melon-like fruit.
The fruit of the watermelon plant, having a green rind and watery flesh that is typically bright red when ripe and contains black pips.
An instrument of attack or defense in combat or hunting, e.g. most guns, missiles, or swords.
An instrument or other means of harming or exerting control over another.
examples: The club that is now mostly used for golf was once a common weapon.
Money is the main weapon of modern oligarchs.

definitions: To perform the marriage ceremony for; to join in matrimony.
To take as one's spouse.
examples: The priest wed the couple.
She wed her first love.

definitions: A day of the week except those which form the weekend; that is:
Any day of the week (Monday through Sunday).
Any of several species of large sea mammals of the infraorder Cetacea.
Something, or someone, that is very large.
To hunt for whales.
To thrash, to flog, to beat vigorously or soundly.
A circular device capable of rotating on its axis, facilitating movement or transportation or performing labour in machines.
The breaking wheel, an old instrument of torture.
examples: the wheel of life

definitions: To roll along on wheels.
To transport something or someone using any wheeled mechanism, such as a wheelchair.
examples: Wheel that trolley over here, would you?
The vulture wheeled above us.

definitions: (interrogative) When ever: emphatic form of when.
At any time.
examples: Whenever will I find time to finish that jigsaw?
Feel free to visit whenever.

definitions: At any time that.
At the (single) time that, no matter when.
examples: Visit whenever you want to.
Tomorrow I'll get up whenever the sun rises.

definitions: An emphatic form of where.
examples: Wherever have you been all my life?

definitions: In or to whatever place; anywhere.
In all places; everywhere.
examples: You can sit wherever you like.
Add quotations wherever they are needed.

definitions: The act of speaking in a quiet voice, especially, without vibration of the vocal cords.
(usually in the plural) A rumor.
examples: There are whispers of rebellion all around.
The soup had just a whisper of basil.

definitions: To speak softly, or under the breath, so as to be heard only by one near at hand; to utter words without sonant breath; to talk without that vibration in the larynx which gives sonorous, or vocal, sound.
To mention privately and confidentially, or in a whisper.
(interrogative) Who ever: an emphatic form of who.
(fused relative) Any person or persons that.
examples: Whoever thought up that stupid idea?
Whoever breaks the law will be punished.

definitions: To become wide or wider.
To make wide or wider.
examples: His eyes widened as her negligee fell to the floor.
She widened his trousers for him.

definitions: The state of being wide.
The measurement of the extent of something from side to side.
examples: Manchester United like to play with width.

definitions: The undomesticated state of a wild animal
(chiefly in the plural) a wilderness
examples: After mending the lion's leg, we returned him to the wild.

definitions: To commit random acts of assault, robbery, and rape in an urban setting, especially as a gang.
Untamed; not domesticated; specifically, in an unbroken line of undomesticated animals (as opposed to feral, referring to undomesticated animals whose ancestors were domesticated).
From or relating to wild creatures.
examples: Przewalski's horses are the only remaining wild horses.
wild honey

definitions: Inaccurately; not on target.
examples: The javelin flew wild and struck a spectator, to the horror of all observing.

definitions: To wish, desire.
To instruct (that something be done) in one's will.
examples: All the fans were willing their team to win the game.
He willed his stamp collection to the local museum.

definitions: The execution of a will.
Ready to do something that is not (can't be expected as) a matter of course.
examples: If my boyfriend isn't willing to change his drinking habits, I will split up with him.

definitions: Fart
Accompanied by wind.
Unsheltered and open to the wind.
examples: It was a long and windy night.
They made love in a windy bus shelter.

definitions: An appendage of an animal's (bird, bat, insect) body that enables it to fly; a similar fin at the side of a ray or similar fish
Human arm.
examples: to take wing
the west wing of the hospital

definitions: To injure slightly (as with a gunshot), especially in the wing or arm.
To fly.
One who has won or often wins.
A point or goal that wins a competition.
Metal formed into a thin, even thread, now usually by being drawn through a hole in a steel die.
A piece of such material; a thread or slender rod of metal, a cable.
examples: This election is going to go right to the wire
to pull the wires for office

definitions: To fasten with wire, especially with reference to wine bottles, corks, or fencing.
To string on a wire.
examples: We need to wire that hole in the fence.
wire beads

definitions: To become wise.
Usually with "up", to inform or learn.
examples: After Mo had a word with him, he wised up.

definitions: Showing good judgement or the benefit of experience.
Disrespectful.
examples: "It is a profitable thing, if one is wise, to seem foolish" - Aeschylus
Don't get wise with me!

definitions: Way, manner, method.
In the context of which the present document or ruling is made.
examples: the within appeal

definitions: In or into the interior; inside.
In the inner part, spatially; physically inside.
In the scope or range of.
examples: within her studio
within his hearing;  her  within five seconds of breaking the record;  within an inch of falling overboard

definitions: Outside, externally. This is still used in the names of some civil parishes in England, e.g. St Cuthbert Without.
Lacking something.
examples: Being from a large, poor family, he learned to live without.

definitions: Outside of, beyond.
Not having, containing, characteristic of, etc.
examples: The snow was swirling without the cottage, but it was warm within.
It was a mistake to leave my house without a coat.

definitions: Unless, except (introducing a clause).
The gray wolf, specifically all subspecies of the gray wolf (Canis lupus) that are not dingoes or dogs.
A man who makes amorous advances to many women.
examples: Check out my pet. He's a wolf.
The soft violin solo was marred by persistent wolves.

definitions: To devour; to gobble; to eat (something) voraciously.
To make amorous advances to many women; to hit on women; to cruise for sex.
Something that causes amazement or awe; a marvel.
Something astonishing and seemingly inexplicable.
examples: Wonders of the World seem to come in sevens.
The idea was so crazy that it is a wonder that anyone went along with it.

definitions: To be affected with surprise or admiration; to be struck with astonishment; to be amazed; to marvel; often followed by at.
To ponder; to feel doubt and curiosity; to query in the mind.
examples: He wondered whether penguins could fly. She had wondered this herself sometimes.

definitions: Tending to excite wonder; surprising, extraordinary.
Surprisingly excellent; very good or admirable, extremely impressive.
examples: They served a wonderful six-course meal.

definitions: Exceedingly, to a great extent.
Made of wood.
As if made of wood; moving awkwardly, or speaking with dull lack of emotion.
examples: On a recent windy day, hundreds of visitors climbed wooden stairs to take pictures in front of the glacier.
wooden acting

definitions: The hair of the sheep, llama and some other ruminants.
A cloth or yarn made from the wool of sheep.
Value.
Merit, excellence.
examples: I’ll have a dollar's worth of candy, please.
Our new director is a man whose worth is well acknowledged.

definitions: Having a value of; proper to be exchanged for.
Deserving of.
examples: A painting worth thousands.
His friendship is not worth having.

definitions: (except in set phrases) To be, become, betide.
examples: Well worth thee, me friend.

definitions: An injury, such as a cut, stab, or tear, to a (usually external) part of the body.
A hurt to a person's feelings, reputation, prospects, etc.
examples: It took a long time to get over the wound of that insult.

definitions: To hurt or injure (someone) by cutting, piercing, or tearing the skin.
To hurt (a person's feelings).
examples: The police officer wounded the suspect during the fight that ensued.
The actor's pride was wounded when the leading role went to his rival.

definitions: A small, usually uncultivated area adjoining or (now especially) within the precincts of a house or other building.
The property surrounding one's house, typically dominated by one's lawn.
examples: I need to hedge a yard of yen.

definitions: To confine to a yard.
The quality or state of being young.
The part of life following childhood; the period of existence preceding maturity or age; the whole early part of life, from childhood, or, sometimes, from infancy, to adulthood.
examples: Her youth and beauty attracted him to her.
I made many mistakes in my youth, but learned from them all.

definitions: Any of three species of genus Equus: E. grevyi, E. quagga, or E. zebra, all with black and white stripes and native to Africa.
A referee.
